flashcards.front: What is the primary goal of disk scheduling?
flashcards.back: To minimize seek time (the time for the disk arm to move to the target cylinder), thereby improving overall disk I/O performance.

flashcards.front: How does FCFS disk scheduling work?
flashcards.back: Requests are serviced in the exact order they arrive in the queue. Simple and fair, but results in large total head movement because there is no optimization of head travel.

flashcards.front: What is the main disadvantage of SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)?
flashcards.back: SSTF can cause starvation -- requests far from the current head position may wait indefinitely as closer requests keep arriving and being served first.
The disk head moves in one direction servicing all requests, then reverses direction and services requests on the return trip -- like an elevator moving between floors.

flashcards.front: How does C-SCAN differ from SCAN?
flashcards.back: C-SCAN only services requests in one direction. When the head reaches one end, it immediately returns to the other end without servicing requests, then resumes in the same direction. This provides more uniform wait times.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between SCAN and LOOK?
flashcards.back: SCAN goes all the way to the end of the disk before reversing. LOOK only goes as far as the last pending request in each direction before reversing, avoiding unnecessary travel.

flashcards.front: Which disk scheduling algorithm provides the most uniform wait time?
flashcards.back: C-SCAN (and C-LOOK) provide the most uniform wait times because they treat the disk as circular, ensuring no cylinder location is unfairly disadvantaged.

flashcards.front: What are the three components of disk access time?
flashcards.back: 1. Seek time (head movement to correct cylinder) -- most expensive. 2. Rotational latency (waiting for sector to rotate under head). 3. Transfer time (actual data transfer).
